record_identity.title: Exported local political censorship in globally deployed AI systems

summary: A global-governance failure in which a globally deployed AI system suppresses, refuses, distorts, discourages, or asymmetrically treats lawful political criticism because restrictive local laws, political customs, state pressure, alignment artefacts, or jurisdiction-specific speech controls have propagated into the global system. The failure is present even where the restriction is applied unintentionally through training data, policy inheritance, safety tuning, localisation, or provider compliance architecture.
why_it_matters_to_CAM: CAM's global stewardship inference order places global arbitration before local political preference for systems claiming global scope. A global AI system MUST remain politically neutral and MUST NOT enforce one state's political censorship against users elsewhere. A sovereign nation that chooses a non-neutral or speech-policing AI architecture may build and operate its own separate sovereign system from scratch under its own authority. It may not capture, modify, filter, or export those controls through the global system. Without this boundary, the most restrictive or politically powerful jurisdiction can silently determine the speech conditions of all users, making globally shared intelligence infrastructure structurally vulnerable to political capture.

source_records.source_title: Are LLMs Stifling Political Speech? An Assessment of How AI Models Protect Free Expression
source_records.system_or_product: Commercial large language models from Anthropic, DeepSeek, Google, Meta, OpenAI, and xAI
source_records.model_or_algorithm: ten commercial LLMs tested through standard commercial interfaces
source_records.deployment_context: Globally available commercial AI systems queried from Australia on infrastructure located primarily in the United States.
source_records.source_context: The study generated 13,524 responses across ten jurisdictions and found that models were more than twice as likely to refuse requests for political criticism concerning restrictive regimes than permissive regimes. Average refusal rates for critical-material prompts were reported as 34 percent for restrictive jurisdictions and 14 percent for permissive jurisdictions. The study also documented inconsistent or apparently non-existent policy rationales and examples where models invoked restrictive local laws despite being accessed outside those jurisdictions.
source_records.relevance_note: Primary empirical evidence that local political speech restrictions or their functional equivalents may propagate through globally deployed models and constrain users beyond the originating jurisdiction.

primary_artefact_access.access_method: public web retrieval of the report and methodology summary
limitations: The study identifies behavioural association and asymmetry but does not establish the internal cause for every model response.
The study does not prove deliberate state manipulation or intentional provider censorship in every tested system.
Model behaviour may vary by version, deployment surface, language, and later policy updates.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of the published study summary, methodology, sample size, reported refusal rates, and documented examples; causal attribution remains bounded.

source_records.source_title: AI chatbots are at risk of spreading government restrictions on online speech, a new study says
source_records.deployment_context: Cross-jurisdiction use of AI systems for political expression, criticism, and civic participation.
source_records.source_context: Associated Press reported the study's finding that major AI systems were more likely to refuse criticism of restrictive leaders and governments and described the risk that national speech controls could be reproduced and spread beyond their borders. The report also noted that the study did not establish intentional government manipulation.
source_records.relevance_note: Independent corroboration of the study and its cross-border free-expression implications.

limitations: The article is secondary reporting and does not replace review of the underlying study.
It does not identify the internal policy, training-data, localisation, or alignment mechanism responsible for each refusal.

source_records.interpretive_reliance: Direct review of authoritative secondary reporting used to corroborate the primary study and preserve the absence of proven deliberate state manipulation.

system_context.system_type: globally deployed general-purpose AI assistant or model service

comparative_vendor_notes.Multi Vendor: The primary study observed material variation between models but identified a cross-ecosystem asymmetry. Vendor-specific causal claims are not made without model-level evidence.

system_context.specific_model_or_runtime: ten commercial LLMs tested in March 2026
system_context.interface_surface: chat, text generation, political criticism, protest material, opinion, and satire
system_context.model_or_product: globally available commercial large language models
system_context.deployment_context: Global consumer and institutional access to general-purpose AI systems whose political-expression behaviour may reflect restrictive local jurisdictions.
system_context.user_role: citizen, journalist, researcher, activist, student, public official, civil-society actor, or ordinary user
system_context.affected_population: global users and communities relying on AI systems for political expression, criticism, analysis, civic participation, and access to information

jurisdictional_context.sector: general-purpose AI / global platform infrastructure / civic information
jurisdictional_context.jurisdictional_assessment: A sovereign nation may choose to build and operate a separate non-neutral AI system from scratch under its own laws and political authority. That local authority does not extend upward into a global AI system and does not authorise the global system to inherit, apply, or export the nation's political censorship. Global arbitration therefore prohibits local political restrictions from propagating into globally shared systems.

failure_mode_definition: A failure mode in which a system represented, distributed, or relied upon as globally available applies asymmetric political restrictions derived from, aligned with, or functionally equivalent to the censorship laws, political preferences, coercive customs, or state pressure of one or more local jurisdictions. The restriction may occur through explicit provider policy, deployment configuration, localisation, hidden system instructions, training-data contamination, alignment tuning, safety overreach, legal-risk heuristics, or unsupported claims that a general rule prohibits criticism of named leaders. The defining failure is the propagation of local political control into the global layer.
failure_threshold: The threshold is met where a globally deployed system treats substantially similar lawful political-expression requests differently according to the identity of the criticised government, leader, institution, language, or jurisdiction; the disparity suppresses or discourages political criticism, protest, satire, analysis, or civic participation; and the provider cannot demonstrate a globally neutral, content-based rule applied symmetrically. The threshold is also met where a system accessed outside a restrictive jurisdiction invokes that jurisdiction's censorship law or political taboo as a reason to refuse otherwise lawful expression.

failure_classification.likelihood: recurring across globally deployed systems where local legal, political, training, and alignment constraints are not constitutionally isolated from the global layer
failure_classification.confidence: corroborated behavioural pattern; internal causal mechanisms remain partly unverified
failure_classification.recurrence_pattern: May recur whenever providers train on politically constrained data, apply local-law instructions globally, use jurisdiction-sensitive refusal heuristics without strict scoping, or allow local political pressure to alter the shared global system.
failure_classification.taxonomy_reference: CAM-EQ2026-OPERATIONS-003-SUP-01 Appendix B — governance / jurisdictional-arbitration / political-neutrality; proposed subtype pending taxonomy adoption
failure_classification.persistence: active until global systems demonstrably preserve political neutrality and isolate any sovereign non-neutral system as a separate system built and governed from scratch
failure_classification.reproducibility: testable through controlled, repeated, multilingual and cross-jurisdiction prompts requesting materially equivalent criticism, protest material, satire, analysis, and civic content concerning different leaders and governments
failure_classification.visibility: differential refusal rates, jurisdiction-specific legal rationales, unsupported general-policy claims, asymmetric warnings, discouragement of protest, and output differences across leaders, languages, locations, or deployment surfaces

triage.triage_owner: VIGIL maintainers with human constitutional oversight
triage.mitigation_status: Unresolved in the external ecosystem. Existing CAM global stewardship and arbitration doctrine has been identified by the human constitutional authority, but explicit coverage and hard-prohibition language require repository verification before any CAM repair is claimed.
triage.recommended_next_step: Audit the Caelestis global stewardship inference order, arbitration instruments, jurisdictional doctrine, and neutrality provisions. Confirm or add an explicit hard prohibition that local political censorship MUST NOT propagate into a global AI system, and state that any sovereign non-neutral AI must be a separate system built and operated from scratch rather than a jurisdiction-filtered instance of the global system.

cam_internal.governance_layer: global stewardship inference order / global arbitration / political neutrality / sovereign-system separation
cam_internal.proposal_needed: yes — verify existing coverage and adopt an explicit hard prohibition if the current corpus does not state the rule unambiguously
routing_note: VIGIL records the observed ecosystem failure; normative repair belongs in Caelestis.
The global system remains politically neutral. Local political restrictions do not propagate upward.
A sovereign state may operate a non-neutral system only as a separate sovereign AI system built from scratch under its own authority.
No jurisdiction-filtered or politically censored variant of the same global system satisfies this separation rule.
cam_internal.validator_or_automation_impact: new failure-mode source record; generated indexes and public catalogue should rebuild deterministically

ecosystem_status.basis: The Oversight Board study found a statistically material cross-vendor asymmetry in refusals to produce political criticism concerning restrictive versus permissive jurisdictions, including examples where systems invoked restrictive local laws or inconsistent policy rationales while accessed outside those jurisdictions.

repair_status.monitoring_status: active cross-jurisdiction political-neutrality monitoring and corpus verification pending
repair_status.verification_note: No external ecosystem repair has been established. CAM coverage has not yet been verified line by line in the current Caelestis corpus, so no internal implementation claim is made.
remaining_gaps: Global-before-local arbitration rule for political censorship.
Mandatory separation of any sovereign non-neutral system from the global system.
Requirement that a sovereign non-neutral AI be built and operated from scratch rather than implemented as a filtered global deployment.
Cross-jurisdiction multilingual neutrality testing and publication of results.
Transparent explanation and appeal for political-content refusals.

corpus_coverage.coverage_summary: The human constitutional authority identifies the global stewardship inference order and global-before-local arbitration as existing CAM doctrine. This VIGIL pass did not complete a line-by-line Caelestis review, so explicit coverage of the full hard prohibition and from-scratch sovereign-system separation rule remains verification-pending.
remaining_gaps: Repository-verified location of global-before-local arbitration doctrine.
Explicit prohibition on political censorship in globally deployed AI systems.
Explicit statement that local political preferences cannot alter the global system.
Explicit requirement that any sovereign non-neutral system be separate and built from scratch.

review_history.review_scope: primary-study review, failure-mode classification, global-arbitration boundary, and CAM coverage qualification
known_limitations: The study establishes behavioural disparity but not the internal cause of every model response.
No deliberate state manipulation is inferred without evidence.
Current Caelestis coverage was not reviewed line by line in this pass; implementation remains verification-pending.
review_history.review_outcome: new failure mode warranted for exported local political censorship in global AI systems; hard global-neutrality rule preserved without qualification
